title: Are Republican Establishment Senators a Security Threat at the Capitol?
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman flips the security narrative used against abolitionists at the Oklahoma Capitol, arguing that State Senators Greg McCortney and Larry Boggs are themselves the violent actors and likening their behavior to Preston Brooks's 1856 caning of Senator Charles Sumner.
audiences: Oklahoma legislator or staffer reading press about Capitol security
Pro-life Christian skeptical of abolitionist tactics
Journalist or fact-checker covering SB13 protests
Abolitionist activist accused of being a 'mob'
History-minded reader interested in antebellum parallels

questions.question: What public claim did Senator Greg McCortney make about abolitionists at the Oklahoma Capitol on the day of the SB13 committee vote?
questions.audience: Journalist or fact-checker covering SB13 protests
questions.answer: McCortney tweeted that the Oklahoma Highway Patrol was present in numbers and that multiple security briefings had been required because Christians (SB13 supporters) were returning to the Capitol, and he called that fact 'embarrassing.'
questions.quote: The @OHPtraffic is here in numbers and there have been multiple security briefings because the Christians are coming back to #OKLeg today. That statement is embarrassing,

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the actual track record of abolitionist conduct at the Capitol?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian skeptical of abolitionist tactics
questions.answer: He insists the abolitionist movement is explicitly non-violent and that no abolitionist has ever caused a security incident at the Capitol or, to his knowledge, even threatened violence against an anti-abolitionist; he invites accusers to produce evidence and pledges to rebuke any abolitionist who has done so.
questions.quote: We are explicitly a non-violent movement. No abolitionist has ever been violent towards an anti-abolitionist.

questions.question: What language did Senator Larry Boggs use to describe SB13 supporters at the July 14th Haskell County GOP meeting?
questions.audience: Oklahoma legislator or staffer reading press about Capitol security
questions.answer: Boggs labeled the abolitionists present at the meeting with a stack of inflammatory descriptors, calling them a violent and radical mob and a group of thugs, despite video evidence Silberman says contradicts those characterizations.
questions.quote: a “violent mob,” a “radical mob”, a “hostile shouting mob,” and a group of “thugs.”

questions.question: What did Senator McCortney reportedly say to abolitionist Sam Riley about fellow State Senator Joseph Silk?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist accused of being a 'mob'
questions.answer: During an otherwise civil sidewalk conversation in Ada, Oklahoma, McCortney told Riley that he would physically assault Silk if Silk appeared at that moment, an admission Silberman points to as a literal threat of violence by the senator himself.
questions.quote: if Joseph Silk walked up here now I’d beat the crap out of him.

questions.question: How did Boggs reportedly respond when abolitionist Daniel Navejas confronted him about voting against SB13?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian skeptical of abolitionist tactics
questions.answer: After Navejas calmly told Boggs that incremental bills perpetuate the slaughter and called him to repent, Boggs lost his temper, swore at Navejas, and challenged him to a fight outside.
questions.quote: Screw you!”  and “If you don’t like it, step outside and you square right up!

questions.question: What historical parallel does Silberman draw to frame the senators' threats?
questions.audience: History-minded reader interested in antebellum parallels
questions.answer: He compares McCortney and Boggs to Preston Brooks, the anti-abolitionist congressman who in 1856 beat Senator Charles Sumner with a cane in Sumner's office after Sumner's blistering Senate-floor speech against slavery and the senators who defended it.
questions.quote: Preston Brooks, who beat slavery abolitionist Senator Charles Sumner with a cane inside Sumner’s office in 1856 following a speech Sumner gave on the Senate floor savaging slave owners and anti-abolition Senators for the sin of chattel slavery.

questions.question: How does Silberman explain why the abolitionist message provokes such intense hostility from politicians?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist accused of being a 'mob'
questions.answer: He argues that calling people to repent of obstructing the abolition of a great evil is inherently offensive, and that the resulting rage is morally chargeable to those delaying justice rather than to those exposing the wrong.
questions.quote: The blame for this rage cannot be assigned to those exposing the evil deeds, but must fall upon those opposing or delaying justice who lash out in pride when called to repent.

questions.question: What conclusion does Silberman draw about who actually warrants increased Capitol security?
questions.audience: Journalist or fact-checker covering SB13 protests
questions.answer: He turns the senators' framing on its head, suggesting that heightened security is in fact warranted to protect abolitionists from Boggs, McCortney, and their allies rather than to protect legislators from peaceful Christian activists.
questions.quote: it would appear that increased security at the capitol is needed, especially when abolitionists are there, to make sure Boggs, McCortney, and their cronies don’t attack abolitionists.

related.title: My Conversation With Senator Greg McCortney
related.reason: Sam Riley's first-person account is the source of the McCortney 'beat the crap out of him' quote that anchors this article's case against the senator.

related.title: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
related.reason: Companion Silberman piece on Boggs that documents his alignment with the same anti-SB13 senators referenced here.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Provides the legislative backdrop for the committee-vote tweet that triggered this article's security-threat critique.

related.title: Watch - Of Giants & Heroes: Joseph Silk
related.reason: Profiles the SB13 champion Joseph Silk, the senator McCortney threatened to assault in the exchange recounted here.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13"?
related.reason: Explains the SB13 strategy that puts abolitionists at the Capitol in the first place, contextualizing the security debate.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the incrementalism critique that Daniel Navejas reportedly delivered to Boggs before Boggs invited him to step outside.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Earlier Silberman indictment of the Republican establishment senators blocking abolition in Oklahoma, of which McCortney and Boggs are exemplars.
